analogy: Think of "Human Capital Formation" through the chapter's own opening images: 75 RURAL DEVELOPMENT After studying this chapter, the learners will • understand rural development and the major issues associated with it • appreciate how crucial the development of rural areas is for India’s overall development • understand the critical role of credit and marketing systems... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 75 RURAL DEVELOPMENT, RURAL, DEVELOPMENT, 76 INDIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Human Capital Formation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 75 RURAL DEVELOPMENT After studying this chapter, the learners will • understand rural development and the major issues associated with it • appreciate how crucial the development of rural areas is for India’s overall development • understand the critical role of credit and marketing systems in rural development • learn about the importance of diversification of productive activities to sustain livelihoods • understand the significance of organic farming in sustainable development.

The text explains that 76 INDIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT 5.1 INTRODUCTION Previously we have studied how poverty was a major challenge facing India.

The text explains that we also know that the majority of the poor live in rural areas where they do not have access to the basic necessities of life.

The text explains that agriculture is the major source of livelihood in the rural sector.

The text explains that mahatma Gandhi once said that the real progress of India did not mean simply the growth and expansion of industrial urban centres but mainly the development of the villages.

The text explains that this idea of village development being at the centre of the overall development of the nation is relevant even today.

The text explains that why should we attach such significance to rural development when we see around us fast growing cities with large industries and modern information technology hubs?

The text explains that it is because more than two-third of India’s population depends on agriculture that is yet to become productive enough to provide for them; one-fourth of rural India still lives in abject poverty.

The text explains that that is the reason why we have to see a developed rural India if our nation has to realise real progress.

The text explains that it essentially focuses on action for the development of areas that are lagging behind in the overall development of the village economy.

The text explains that some of the areas which are challenging and need fresh initiatives for development in rural India include • Development of human resources including – literacy, more specifically, female literacy, education and skill development – health, addressing both sanitation and public health • Land reforms • Development of the productive resources of each locality • Infrastructure development like electricity, irrigation, credit, marketing, transport facilities including construction of village roads and feeder roads to nearby highways, facilities for agriculture research and extension, and information dissemination • Special measures for alleviation of poverty and bringing about significant improvement in the living conditions of the weaker sections of the population emphasising access to productive employment opportunities All this means that people engaged in farm and non-farm activities in rural areas have to be provided with various means that help them increase the productivity.

The text explains that they also need to be given opportunities to diversify into various non-farm productive “Only the tillers of the soil live by the right.

The text explains that the rest form their train and eat only the bread of dependence”.

The text explains that 77 RURAL DEVELOPMENT activities such as food processing.

The text explains that enabling them better and more affordable access to healthcare, sanitation facilities at workplaces and homes and education for all would also need to be given top priority for rapid rural development.

The text explains that it was observed in an earlier chapter that although the share of agriculture sector’s contribution to GDP was on a decline, the population dependent on this sector did not show any significant decline.

The text explains that scholars identify decline in public investment since 1991 as the major reason for this.

The text explains that they also argue that inadequate infrastructure, lack of alternate employment opportunities in the industry or service sector, increasing casualisation of employment etc., further impede rural development.

The text explains that against this background, we will critically look at some of the crucial aspects of rural India like credit and marketing systems, agricultural diversification and the role of organic farming in promoting sustainable development.

The text explains that 5.3 CREDIT AND MARKETING IN RURA L AREAS Credit: Growth of rural economy depends primarily on infusion of capital, from time to time, to realise higher productivity in agriculture and non-agriculture sectors.

The text explains that as the time of gestation period between crop sowing and realisation of income after production is quite long, farmers borrow from various sources to meet their initial investment on seeds, fertilisers, implements and other family expenses of marriage, death, religious ceremonies etc.

The text explains that discuss this in the classroom. ÿ Prepare a list of recent schemes and their objectives from the government website https:// www.rural.nic.in.

The text explains that in 1995, a thrift and credit society was started as a small savings bank for poor women with the objective to encourage savings.

The text explains that the thrift and credit society mobilised Rs 1 crore as thrift savings.

The text explains that national Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD) was set up in 1982 as an apex body to coordinate the activities of all institutions involved in the rural financing system.

The text explains that the Green Revolution was a harbinger of major changes in the credit system as it led to the diversification of the portfolio of rural credit towards production- oriented lending.

The text explains that the institutional structure of rural banking today consists of a set of multi-agency institutions, namely, commercial banks, regional rural banks (RRBs), cooperatives and land development banks.

The text explains that recently, Self-Help Groups (henceforth SHGs) have emerged to fill the gap in the formal credit system because the formal credit delivery mechanism has not only proven inadequate but has also not been fully integrated into the overall rural social and community development.

The text explains that from the pooled money, credit is given to the needy members to be repayable in small instalments at reasonable interest rates.

The text explains that identify the reasons for not starting self employment activities and discuss in the classroom.

The text explains that rural Banking — a Critical Appraisal: Rapid expansion of the banking system had a positive effect on rural farm and non-farm output, income and employment, especially after the green revolution — it helped farmers to avail services and credit facilities and a variety of loans for meeting their prodution needs.

The text explains that however, all is not well with our banking system.

The text explains that 10,000 and get their wages if they get any government-related jobs and works under MNREGA (now known as Viksit Bharat – Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission [Gramin] [VB-G RAM G]); old age pension and Work These Out ÿ In the last few years, you might have taken note — in your neighbourhood if you are living in rural areas or read in the newspapers or seen on TV — of farmers commiting suicides.

The text explains that collect details such as how many rural households borrowed from them, amount generally borrowed, kinds of collateral used, interest rates and dues. ÿ If farmers who borrowed from cooperative banks could not pay back due to crop failure and other reasons, their loans should be waived otherwise they may take drastic decisions like committing suicides.

The text explains that 80 INDIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT other social security payments of the government are transferred to bank accounts.

The text explains that this has led to more than 50 crore people opening bank accounts; indirectly it has promoted thrift habit and efficient allocation of financial resources particularly in rural areas.

The text explains that 5.4 AGRICULTURAL MARKET SYSTEM Have you ever asked yourself how food grains, vegetables and fruits that we consume daily come from different parts of the country?

The text explains that agricultural marketing is a process that involves the assembling, storage, processing, transportation, packaging, grading and distribution of different agricultural commodities across the country.

The text explains that therefore, government intervention became necessary to regulate the activities of the private traders.

The text explains that second component is provision of physical infrastructure facilities like roads, railways, warehouses, godowns, cold storages and processing units.

The text explains that the current infrastructure facilities are quite inadequate to meet the growing demand and need to be improved.

The text explains that 81 RURAL DEVELOPMENT marketing, in realising fair prices for farmers’ products, is the third aspect of government initiative.

The text explains that however cooperatives have received a setback during the recent past due to inadequate coverage of farmer members, lack of appropriate link between marketing and processing cooperatives and inefficient financial management.

The text explains that however, despite government intervention, private trade (by moneylenders, rural political elites, big merchants and rich farmers) predominates agricultural markets.

The text explains that the Indian Economy: Problems and Perspectives.

The text explains that nABARD Occasional Paper No: 38, Department of Agriculture and Rural Development, Mumbai.

The text explains that horticulture Development, State of the Indian Farmer, a Millennium Study.

The text explains that agricultural Credit in India: State of the Indian Farmer, a Millennium Study.

The text explains that organic Vegetable Gardening: Grow Your Own Vegetables.

The text explains that unit for Labour Studies, Tata Institute of Social Sciences, Mumbai.

The text explains that government Reports Successful Governance Initiatives and Best Practices: Experiences from Indian States, Government of India in Coordination with Human Resource Development Centre and UNDP, Planning Commission, Delhi, 2002.

The text explains that annual Reports, Ministry of Rural Development, Government of India, New Delhi.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Economic Development, Chapter 5: Human Capital Formation

prelims_traps: Do not treat 75 RURAL DEVELOPMENT and RURAL as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Human Capital Formation; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 75 RURAL DEVELOPMENT and RURAL to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
